dccWarningBody: If a merchant or ATM offers to charge you in Thai Baht (THB), always decline and choose to pay in the local currency instead. Accepting DCC lets the merchant set the exchange rate, which is typically far worse than your card network rate.
estimationNote: Calculations are estimates based on mid-market rates and typical card fee tiers. Actual rates charged by your card issuer may vary.

guideDccIntlTitle: What is DCC and why should you avoid it?
guideDccExplanation: This is when a merchant or ATM offers to charge you in your home currency instead of the local currency. It sounds convenient, but the exchange rate is set by the merchant — which is typically 3%–10% worse than the true market rate.
costExampleNote: This example shows how ATM fees and foreign transaction fees can increase your travel costs.
costExampleTip: Tip: Withdraw larger amounts fewer times to reduce ATM fees.
guideCalculatorDesc: Use the calculator below to estimate FX fees when paying in this country.
tmFxLossTitle: How Much Do You Lose on FX Fees in
tmFxLossDesc: Most travelers silently pay 3–6% more than the fair exchange rate every time they pay by card or withdraw from an ATM abroad. The cost comes from two sources stacked on top of each other:
tmFxFeeDesc: charged by your card issuer (typically 2.5–3.5% for a standard bank card).
tmSpreadDesc: the gap between the mid-market rate and what Visa/Mastercard charge (typically 0.5–1.5%).
tmDccDesc: an optional trap where the merchant converts to your home currency at a rate 3–8% worse. Always decline.
tmFxTableTitle: Typical Hidden FX Fees When Paying in
tmSpreadFootnote: Spread percentages are estimates based on typical Visa/Mastercard network rates. Your card issuer's actual fee may differ — check your card terms.
tmExampleDesc: Below is how much extra you pay (above the mid-market rate) depending on your payment method. These are approximate values based on typical fee structures.
tmActualCosts: Actual costs depend on your card issuer and the current mid-market rate.

learnTravelGuideDesc: ATM fees · best cards · typical costs · attractions
tmTip1Body: Airport exchange counters often charge 10–15% above mid-market rate. Exchange only enough for transport; use ATMs or your card for the rest.
tmTip2Body: Cards like Wise, Revolut, or Charles Schwab waive the typical 2.5–3% FX fee entirely, saving you real money on every purchase abroad.
tmTip3Body: When a terminal asks 'Pay in USD or local?' — choose local. Dynamic Currency Conversion (DCC) is marked up 3–8% and benefits the merchant, not you.
tmTip4Body: Network ATMs (Citibank, HSBC) often offer better rates and lower fees than independent FX ATMs. Decline any 'fixed fee' conversion offer.
tmTip5Body: In markets like Japan (PayPay), China (Alipay), or Thailand (PromptPay), local mobile payments sometimes give you mid-market rate with no markup.
tmTip6Body: Mid-market rates fluctuate daily. Knowing the fair rate before you travel helps you spot a bad deal — and walk away from it.
